mnemonics.title: B-R-U-T-E Force Key Points
mnemonics.content:
B - Baseline comparison algorithm
R - Recursive/iterative exploration
U - Unguided search (no heuristics)
T - Tries ALL possibilities
E - Exhaustive (complete search)

mnemonics.title: When to Use Brute Force
mnemonics.content: SMALL P: Size small, Memory available, Algorithmic simplicity, Ladder to better solutions needed, Practical when hardware handles it, Problem has no better known solution

acronyms.usage: Find shortest Hamiltonian cycle visiting all cities exactly once
Optimization technique to prune exhaustive search tree
When exact solution is infeasible, try approximate approaches

visualTips.visualization: Draw a tree where root is empty set, each level adds one element, leaf nodes are complete subsets. Total leaf nodes = 2^n shows exponential growth visually.
Imagine balloons rising - largest elements bubble up to top in each pass. After pass 1, largest at end; after pass 2, second largest, etc.
Think of a decision tree at each position: pick any remaining element, recurse for remaining positions. Level 1: n choices, Level 2: n-1 choices, etc.

analogies.analogy: Trying every key on a keyring to find which one opens the lock - guaranteed to work but may take long time.
Exploring a maze: when you hit a dead end, you go back to last junction and try different path instead of continuing into wall.
Finding shortest route: if you already found a 100km route, don't explore branches that must be >100km - prune them early.
Brute-force: trying every possible meal combination at buffet. Greedy: always picking the most expensive-looking dish first.
